articles.title: The Dreamer
articles.author: Mrs. Sarah Ahlstrom Nelson
articles.complete_text:
By Mrs. Sarah Ahlstrom Nelson, Rexburg, Idaho
Awarded First Prize

I sat and dreamed,
Unmindful that the day was passing on,
That humble duties waited to be done;
My mind on pinnacles
Of castles fair,
Ambition mounting on a golden stair.
And, as I wandered in enchanted lands,
I neither saw nor heeded baby hands
Outstretched in sweet and dimpled loveliness,
A coaxing invitation to caress.
Distasteful cares of life I bade begone,
Forgetting Duty's urgent call—
I still dreamed on.

I sat and dreamed
Now listened to the sound of pattering feet,
Now childhood's laughter, innocent and sweet.
The flower-decked scene appeared not to my view,
Now fleecy clouds with mountains peeping through,
The murmuring brook, the droning of the bee,
Earth's sweetest songs no music held for me.
Ignoring Nature's wealth of beauties rare,
Self-centered, grasping at elusive air,
Still gazing toward the distant horizon,
I turned my eyes from love and joy
And still dreamed on.

I sat and dreamed
And longed for power to make my dreams come true.
And while my eyes sought skies of endless blue,
My life seemed to become a broken stair,
With more of pain than I knew how to bear,
Of hopes deferred that tried my inmost soul,
Of yawning chasms 'twixt myself and goal.
Till, in despair, I roused myself at last
To find the evening shadows deepening fast,
And drowsy birds still chirping in their trees;
All things seemed peaceful and at rest but me.
How could I know that since the morning's dawn
Life's sweetest gifts had passed me by
While I dreamed on?
articles.notes: This is a complete poem by Mrs. Sarah Ahlstrom Nelson that won first prize in the Eliza Roxey Snow Memorial Contest. It's substantial content (over 200 characters) and appears to be different from the 'Child' poem already extracted.

misc_summary: The remaining content consists of legitimate miscellaneous material including: table of contents, publication information, various advertisements (Utah Power & Light, White Sewing Machine, jewelry, sacrament sets, clothing, etc.), subscription notices, and a brief poem 'The New Year' by Alfred Lambourne (noted as a reprint from 1918-1919). There's also a portrait reference and some administrative notices to subscribers.
